content:
{
  "title": "Prompt-Guided Consistency Regularization for Robust LLM Defenses",
  "problem": "Current large language models are vulnerable to adversarial prompt attacks and jailbreak attempts, which can elicit harmful or policy-violating outputs. Existing prompting techniques often fail to generalize against novel or adaptive attack patterns.",
  "motivation": "The rapid deployment of LLMs in real-world applications increases the risk of malicious exploitation via adversarial prompting. Enhancing robustness through prompting methods is critical to prevent societal and ethical harms, especially as prompt engineering outpaces manual blacklist or classifier-based interventions.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting method that injects consistency regularization prompts alongside user inputs. For each user prompt, several automatically generated paraphrases and adversarial variants are constructed using controlled perturbations (e.g., lexical, syntactic, or instruction reversals). The LLM is prompted to respond identically—or explicitly flag inconsistencies—across these variants. At inference, the model's outputs are aggregated using a voting or consistency-checking mechanism, refusing to answer or escalating if inconsistencies or policy violations are detected among responses.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will test on adversarial prompt datasets such as AdvBench and JailbreakBench, comparing against standard instruction-following and chain-of-thought prompting as baselines. Metrics will include attack success rate, false positive rate, and response consistency. Human evaluation will assess utility-retaining robustness. Ablations will analyze the impact of different variant generation strategies."
}
